content.problemStatement:
Given a binary search tree, where exactly two nodes of the tree were swapped by mistake. Recover the tree without changing its structure.

Example:




Input Format

The first line of the input contains s single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary search tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Complete the given function by recovering the BST without modifying its structure and return the root of the BST.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the BST. The next N-1 lines each contain three space‑separated values: pi ci Ri, where pi is the parent node index, ci is the child node index, and Ri is either 'L' (ci is the left child of pi) or 'R' (ci is the right child of pi).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the line: OK! BST recovered. Well done!

hidden.label: stress case
hidden.expectedOutput:
OK! BST recovered. Well done!
OK! BST recovered. Well done!

hidden.label: stress case 2
hidden.expectedOutput:
OK! BST recovered. Well done!
OK! BST recovered. Well done!
OK! BST recovered. Well done!

hidden.label: stress case 3
hidden.expectedOutput:
OK! BST recovered. Well done!
OK! BST recovered. Well done!

solution.approach: The inorder traversal of a correct BST yields a strictly increasing sequence of node values. When exactly two nodes are swapped, the inorder sequence has exactly two violations: either two adjacent elements are out of order (one violation) or two distant elements are out of order (two violations). By performing a single inorder walk we keep track of the previous visited node. The first time we see prev.val > cur.val we mark the previous node as the first misplaced node; we also mark the current node as the second misplaced node (it may be updated later). After the traversal we simply swap the values of the two recorded nodes. This restores the BST without altering its shape. The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(H) recursion stack (or O(1) extra space with Morris traversal).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of nodes in the tree.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(H) auxiliary stack space due to recursion, where H is the height of the tree (worst‑case O(N)).
